title: Assessment: Hash Maps — The Universal Speed-Up

questions.question: Why does a hash map guarantee O(1) average-case lookup rather than O(1) worst-case?

options.A: Hash maps use binary search internally, which is guaranteed O(log n)
options.B: A poorly designed hash function can map many keys to the same bucket, forcing O(n) traversal of a collision chain
options.C: The Python dict implementation guarantees O(1) in all cases through open addressing
options.D: Hash maps are O(1) worst-case because each slot holds exactly one value

questions.explanation: Hash maps rely on a good hash function to distribute keys uniformly. In pathological cases (all keys hash to the same bucket), lookup degrades to O(n) linked-list traversal. With a good hash function and load-factor management, expected case is O(1).

questions.question: Given `nums = [2, 7, 11, 15]` and `target = 9`, trace the one-pass Two Sum algorithm. What is stored in the hash map after processing the element 7?

options.D: The algorithm already returned [0, 1] before reaching 7

questions.explanation: When processing nums[1]=7: check if target-7=2 is in the map. {2:0} is already in the map. Return [0, 1]. The algorithm terminates before 7 is stored, because the complement was found immediately.

questions.question: You need to group a list of strings by their sorted characters (anagram groups). Which hash map pattern applies?

options.A: Complement lookup — store each string and check if its complement exists
options.B: Frequency count — count character occurrences and compare counts
options.C: Group-by — use sorted(word) as key; append each word to the corresponding list
options.D: De-duplicate — use a set to track which strings have been seen

questions.explanation: Group-by pattern: transform each element into a canonical key (sorted letters), accumulate all elements sharing that key into a list. `from collections import defaultdict; d=defaultdict(list); d[tuple(sorted(w))].append(w)`.

questions.question: What output does `from collections import Counter; Counter('aabbc')` produce?
questions.explanation: Counter produces a dict-like object mapping each element to its count. 'a' appears twice, 'b' twice, 'c' once. This is the standard frequency-count pattern for character frequency problems.

questions.question: Your solution checks `if x in seen_list:` inside a loop over n elements. The interviewer says 'great logic, but this is O(n²).' What single change fixes this?

options.A: Replace `seen_list` with `seen_set = set()` and use `if x in seen_set:`
options.B: Sort `seen_list` before the loop so membership checks become O(log n)
options.C: Replace the list with a deque for O(1) popleft
options.D: Use `any(x == item for item in seen_list)` for cleaner code

questions.explanation: Set membership `x in set` is O(1) average (hash-based). List membership `x in list` is O(n) (linear scan). Converting to a set before the loop makes the outer loop O(n) × O(1) = O(n) total.

questions.question: When should you reach for `defaultdict(list)` instead of a regular `dict`?

options.A: When you need O(1) lookup for individual keys
options.B: When you are grouping items under keys that may not exist yet, avoiding KeyError on first assignment
options.C: When you need to count occurrences — defaultdict(list) initialises counts to zero
options.D: defaultdict is slower than dict; only use it when code clarity is more important than performance

questions.explanation: defaultdict(list) auto-creates an empty list for missing keys, so `d[key].append(value)` works without checking `if key in d` first. defaultdict(int) (not list) initialises to 0 for counting. Performance is equivalent to dict.

questions.question: Problem: find the longest subarray with equal numbers of 0s and 1s. You convert 0s to -1s and use a prefix sum approach. What role does a hash map play in the O(n) solution?

options.A: It stores the frequencies of each prefix sum for counting
options.B: It maps each prefix sum to the earliest index where that sum was seen, enabling O(1) subarray length calculation
options.C: It replaces the prefix sum array for O(1) space
options.D: It is not needed — the prefix sum array alone is sufficient

questions.explanation: When prefix[i] == prefix[j] (both equal k), the subarray nums[j+1..i] has sum 0 (equal 1s and -1s). Storing `first_seen[prefix] = index` lets you compute subarray length as i - first_seen[prefix[i]] in O(1).

questions.question: Describe the three main hash map patterns covered in this module (complement lookup, frequency count, group-by) and give one interview problem that exemplifies each.

required_points: Complement lookup: store seen values; for each new element, check if (target - element) exists. Example: Two Sum.
Frequency count: map each element to its occurrence count. Example: Valid Anagram, Ransom Note.
Group-by: map a canonical key to a list of matching elements. Example: Group Anagrams.
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any correct LeetCode problem for each category. Counter pattern is a sub-case of frequency count.
answer_key.full_credit: All three patterns named accurately with a correct example problem each
answer_key.partial_credit: Two patterns correctly named and exemplified

questions.question: Explain why using a Python list as a queue inside BFS is a time complexity bug, and show the two-line fix.

required_points: list.pop(0) removes the first element in O(n) because all remaining elements shift left
Over V vertices, total BFS cost becomes O(V×n) = O(n²) instead of O(V+E)
Fix: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); use queue.popleft() — O(1) both operations
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May show code instead of prose for the fix
answer_key.full_credit: Identifies O(n) pop cost, explains the compounding effect, provides deque fix
answer_key.partial_credit: Identifies the problem without quantifying or without providing the fix

questions.prompt: A colleague claims that hash maps are a 'brute force shortcut' that avoids real algorithmic thinking. Evaluate this claim. When does adding a hash map represent elegant algorithm design, and when might it obscure a more efficient solution?
questions.guidance: A strong response shows: when O(n) hash map trades space for time correctly (Two Sum, subarray problems); when it is genuinely the optimal approach; and one case where a different technique (sorting + two pointers, sliding window) avoids the hash map's O(n) space overhead.

rubric.A: Defends the hash map's role with 2+ concrete examples, identifies the space tradeoff, and gives a legitimate alternative that avoids the hash map
rubric.B: Defends the hash map with examples but doesn't identify a case where an alternative is better
rubric.C: Either dismisses the claim without examples or agrees with it without nuance
